title: Rust basics
description: Ownership, borrowing, modules, enums, the type system. Every question maps onto a pattern this site's own crate uses, with a follow-up link to the relevant lesson.

questions.prompt: In Rust, if you write `let v = vec![1, 2, 3]; let w = v;`, what happens to `v` after the second line?
options: `v` is moved into `w`; using `v` after the assignment is a compile error.
`v` is copied into `w`; both variables now point to independent vectors.
`v` is shared with `w`; both variables refer to the same vector via shared ownership.
`v` is dropped immediately when `w` is bound.
questions.explanation: Vec<T> does not implement Copy, so binding it to a new variable moves it. The compiler then refuses to let you use the old binding. Primitives like i32 and f64 do implement Copy, which is why `let x = 5; let y = x;` lets you keep using x.

questions.prompt: Why do all the strategy functions in this site's crate take `bars: &[DailyBar]` instead of `bars: Vec<DailyBar>`?
options: Because Vec<DailyBar> would not compile in WASM.
Because borrowing avoids taking ownership of the bar series, letting the caller keep using it after the strategy returns.
Because slices are faster to iterate than Vec.
Because the WASM bridge cannot pass a Vec across the boundary.
questions.explanation: The strategy doesn't need to own the bars; it just reads them. Borrowing with &[T] lets the caller keep the Vec alive and reuse it for other strategies in the same backtest session. Vec<DailyBar> would compile fine but the move would force a clone if the caller wanted to keep using the original.

questions.prompt: `StrategyKind` is declared as `enum StrategyKind { BuyAndHold, SmaCrossover(Params), ... }`. What does the inner `Params` actually mean here?
options: It's a base class that all strategy params inherit from.
It's a generic type parameter that gets monomorphized per variant.
It's a concrete struct, scoped to the strategy's module, that wraps that variant's parameters.
It's a serde attribute that controls JSON serialization.
questions.explanation: Each strategy submodule defines its own `pub struct Params`. The enum variant wraps that strategy-specific struct as a tuple variant; the variant name plus the struct shape combine to make the dispatch unambiguous on both the Rust side and the JSON wire format.

questions.prompt: The crate declares `pub mod strategies;` in `lib.rs`. Where does the Rust compiler look for the contents of that module?
options: Inside `lib.rs` itself, between matching curly braces.
At `src/strategies.rs` or `src/strategies/mod.rs`.
Inside `Cargo.toml`'s `[modules]` section.
At a path declared via the `#[path = "..."]` attribute (required for sub-modules).
questions.explanation: Rust's module system follows the filename convention. `pub mod strategies;` resolves to either `src/strategies.rs` (a single-file module) or `src/strategies/mod.rs` (a directory module with submodules). This site uses the directory form because the strategies submodule has its own submodules per strategy.

questions.prompt: `positions()` returns `Result<Vec<f64>, BacktestError>`. Why `Result` and not `Option<Vec<f64>>` or just `Vec<f64>`?
options: Result is faster than Option in WASM.
Option can only wrap a single value; Vec needs Result.
Result carries the reason for failure (`BacktestError` describes WHY positions couldn't be computed), where Option would only say a value was absent.
Result is required for any function that allocates a Vec.
questions.explanation: When a strategy can't compute positions, the caller needs to know whether it was bad parameters, too few bars, or something else. Option<Vec<f64>> would just say `None` with no detail. Result<T, E> carries the error type explicitly; here that's `BacktestError` which itself is an enum of specific failure shapes.

questions.prompt: `DailyBar` is declared with `#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]`. What does the `Serialize` derive actually do at compile time?
options: It writes a function to disk that serializes the struct.
It generates an `impl Serialize for DailyBar` block that walks the struct's fields and calls `serializer.serialize_*` for each one.
It marks the struct for runtime reflection so serde can introspect it.
Nothing at compile time; it tags the type for serialization at runtime.
questions.explanation: Derive macros run at compile time and generate code that the compiler then compiles alongside your own code. Serialize generates the `serialize` method body by walking the struct definition; the output looks like hand-written code that calls serializer methods for each field. No runtime reflection involved.

questions.prompt: Where do the unit tests in `crates/backtest-core/src/metrics.rs` actually live in the file, and what attribute marks them?
options: In a separate file `tests/metrics.rs`, marked with `#[test]` on each function.
Inline in metrics.rs inside a `mod tests` block annotated `#[cfg(test)]`, with `#[test]` on each test function.
Inside `target/test/` after `cargo test` generates them.
In `metrics.rs` but only inside `#[cfg(debug_assertions)]` blocks.
questions.explanation: Rust's convention is per-module test submodules: a `mod tests` block inside each source file with `#[cfg(test)]` so it compiles only under `cargo test`. Each test function is marked `#[test]`. The integration-test variant in `tests/` exists too but is for testing the public API across multiple modules; per-module unit tests stay close to the code they exercise.
